risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Random assignment was performed by means of numbered cards in envelopes for the intervention and the control group, and was stratified by age group (<, ⩾60 years)." Comment: Numbered cards in envelopes indicates an allocation sequence generated from a random method; although the paper does not name the specific random-number method, the description is sufficient to judge the sequence generation as truly random rather than predictable.

Quote: "Random assignment was performed by means of numbered cards in envelopes for the intervention and the control group" Comment: The paper explicitly reports a numbered-envelope allocation procedure. While it does not spell out opaque/sealed in full, the described numbered cards in envelopes indicates a structured allocation mechanism intended to conceal the next assignment from recruiters, and no open list or predictable method is described.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Blinding was not possible within the hospital procedures. Nonetheless, in most cases physicians did not know in which arm patients had been randomised." Comment: Participants were necessarily aware of receiving the 20-minute decision aid plus brochure versus standard care, and the paper explicitly states blinding was not possible. Because the main outcomes included patient-reported decisional conflict, perceived involvement, satisfaction, and consultation-related behaviors—outcomes that could plausibly be influenced by knowledge of allocation—the study is at high risk of performance bias overall.

Quote: "Blinding was not possible within the hospital procedures. Nonetheless, in most cases physicians did not know in which arm patients had been randomised." Comment: Outcome assessment was largely by patient self-report (e.g. decisional conflict, perceived involvement in care, satisfaction, length of consultation, time point of decision making), so the patient was effectively the assessor for most outcomes. Because participants were aware of their intervention condition and the main subjective outcomes could be influenced by that knowledge, detection bias is likely.

Quote: "The final sample was 111 patients, of whom 55 were randomly assigned to the intervention group and 56 to the control group. Here, 13 and 16 patients respectively were excluded from the analyses because of post hoc ascertainment of not having met the inclusion criteria (benign diagnosis, carcinoma in situ, Stage IV-tumour)." Comment: Attrition/missing outcome data appear minimal and balanced after randomization: the main loss from the randomized sample was due to post hoc ineligibility exclusions rather than differential dropout, and the remaining analyzed groups were similar in size. The paper reports follow-up at 1 week and presents complete outcome tables for the analyzed sample, with no signal that missing data were imbalanced or outcome-related.
